retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The facial artery has a cervical division that gives rise to 4 other vessels before supplying the face. The facial artery's cervical branches include the ascending palatine, tonsillar, submental, and glandular branches. Meanwhile, the main vessels arising from the facial artery's trunk include the inferior and superior labial, lateral nasal (supplies the nasalis), and angular arteries. [1] The angular artery is the facial artery's terminal segment.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The facial artery continues its journey superiorly at an oblique angle across the cheek. Afterward, the vessel approaches the oral commissure, ascends adjacent to the nasal sidewall, and culminates at the medial eye canthus, where it continues as the angular artery. This elaborate pathway underscores the facial artery's essential function in facial vascularization and integration within the head and neck's broader circulatory system. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Flap Design -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Anatomical Structures of the Maxilla
retrieved_docs.content: The facial artery, a proximal branch of the external carotid system, supplies the superficial structures of the face, including facial expression muscles, skin, and facial fat and fascia. This artery crosses the mandible at the gonial notch to enter the face, traveling deeply along the surfaces of the buccinator and levator anguli oris muscles as it approaches the oral commissure. At this point, it divides into the inferior and superior labial arteries, with the superior labial artery giving rise to the angular artery, which supplies the lateral nose (see Image. Anatomy of Facial Artery).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The facial artery is a major vessel originating from the external carotid artery (ECA) that primarily supplies the face's superficial structures (see Image. Facial Artery). This artery ascends from the neck to the face, traversing significant anatomical landmarks, such as the mandible and the mouth's angle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The facial artery's pulse is typically palpable at the masseter's anteroinferior angle against the mandible's bony surface, where it runs superficially. However, strong pulsations of the angular artery, the facial artery's terminal branch, may be appreciated in around 70% of cases where the ICA is occluded. The angular artery connects with the infraorbital artery's medial branches externally and the ophthalmic artery's dorsal nasal and supratrochlear branches internally through anastomotic channels. However, the diagnostic significance of this finding is limited, as the angular artery palpability without ICA occlusion is normal in only 1 out of 10 adults older than 55 with long-standing hypertension. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The nerve innervation of the face will also originate from the first and second pharyngeal arches. The first pharyngeal arch will form the trigeminal nerve, while the second pharyngeal arch develops the facial nerve. As for the blood supply to the face, the third aortic arch forms the common carotid artery, which divided into the internal and external carotid arteries as it elongates. As the external carotid artery elongates, it further branches into the superior thyroid, lingual, facial, ascending pharyngeal, occipital, posterior auricular, and superficial temporal arteries. Uniquely, the first aortic arch forms the maxillary artery that perfuses the deeper structures of the face. The maxillary anastomoses with the external carotid artery becoming one of the terminal arterial branches of the external carotid artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Labial Artery -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: In plastic surgery, the facial artery's pulsatile motion may create an undesirable appearance unilaterally. The ligation of one facial artery usually causes no severe consequences because of the collateral blood flow from the contralateral facial artery's branches. [7] Another cosmetic procedure is lip fillers, with the knowledge of the anatomy of the labial arteries, which can provide maximally aesthetic appearance with optimal safety. Since the labial artery is deep in the lips, fillers may be injected superficially without damaging the underlying arteries. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Labial Artery -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The labial arteries are direct branches from the facial arteries. The labial arteries can further subdivide into the superior labial arteries and inferior labial arteries. The superior labial arteries are larger than the inferior labial arteries. The labial arteries branch from the facial artery at the region where the vermillion border of the upper lip meets the vermillion border of the lower lip. The superior labial artery traverses between the mucosal and upper part of the orbicularis oris muscle. The inferior labial artery traverses between the mucosal and lower part of the orbicularis oris muscle. As the labial arteries traverse the lips, they will anastomose with the labial arteries of the contralateral side. The anastomosis forms collateral blood flow for the lips and underlying muscles.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Labial Artery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The lips receive most of their blood supply from the labial arteries. Due to the various branches, the labial arteries can efficiently provide blood to the lips. Aside from providing blood to the lips for proper function, the blood flow also contributes to the color in the lips. Adequate blood supply is required to maintain appropriate functioning, including breathing, eating, and facial expression.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, the facial artery's palpable pulse at specific points along its course serves as a vital diagnostic tool for healthcare professionals, aiding in the assessment of circulatory health and facilitating accurate diagnoses of vascular conditions. In surgical contexts, the facial artery's precise anatomical knowledge is paramount during cervicofacial procedures. Surgeons rely on a thorough understanding of this vessel's course to navigate safely during operations, minimizing the risk of inadvertent damage and ensuring optimal outcomes. Understanding the facial artery's anatomy and function is essential in treating various cervicofacial disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The facial artery navigates underneath the posterior digastric belly and stylohyoid in its ascent across the facial region. Afterward, the vessel courses along the submandibular gland's posterior aspect, arches over the mandibular body, and advances along the masseter's anteroinferior border. The artery's passage near the mandible is clinically significant due to its palpable pulse at this site. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The hypoglossal nerve travels deep to the proximal facial artery. The facial artery often traverses deep to the facial nerve's marginal mandibular branch. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The primary blood supply to the face derives from the external carotid artery and the branches of the external carotid artery. As the common carotid artery ascends cranially, it branches into the internal and external carotid arteries. The external carotid artery further branches into superior thyroid, lingual, facial, ascending pharyngeal, occipital, posterior auricular, maxillary, and superficial temporal arteries. The facial, superficial temporal, and maxillary arteries are the main vessels that will provide perfusion to the face. The superficial temporal artery travels toward the temporal and forehead region. The superficial temporal artery perfuses the structures mainly in the temporal and forehead territories. The facial artery is responsible for perfusing the majority of the face. The facial artery travels toward the nose and lips, while the maxillary artery provides some perfusion to the cheek region. The maxillary artery travels toward the deep structures and perfusing the deeper facial structures. [3] [4] As for the venous drainage, the face drains the majority of the venous blood via the facial vein. The facial vein eventually drains the blood back into the internal jugular vein.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: CCA atherosclerosis increases the risk of head and neck embolic events, leading to ischemic injury. Facial artery occlusion may lead to superficial facial muscle weakness. Rarely, CCA emboli may travel and cause ischemic injury to distal facial artery branches.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ophthalmic Arteries -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The Supraorbital Artery = Branches from the ophthalmic artery during its course over the optic nerve, passes anteriorly along the medial border of the levator palpebrae and superior rectus, and then through the supraorbital foramen to provide blood supply to the muscles and skin of the forehead.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1892
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall intracranial arterial architecture. This anatomical framework also serves as a common site for the finding of تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis), highlighting the importance of evaluating the basilar artery and related intracranial vessels for sclerotic changes that can impact cerebral blood flow and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 198
retrieved_docs.content: procedure site, highlighting the relevance of these venous access routes for procedures that ultimately involve the pulmonary artery. The Structure of intracranial artery has a finding site of تصلب عصيدي في المخ, signifying the presence of intracranial atherosclerosis, and it is an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck. This relationship suggests that endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels can affect intracranial arterial structures, linking vascular surgical interventions to intracranial vascular pathology. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how a hierarchical set of arterial therapeutic interventions, catheterization techniques, and vascular findings are interconnected within the broader framework of vascular medicine, providing clinicians with a comprehensive view of procedural options and their anatomical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1026
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall architecture of intracranial arterial structures. This anatomical framework serves as a common site for the finding described as تصلب عصيدي في المخ, reflecting the presence of intracranial atherosclerotic disease within these vessels. Surgical management of vascular pathology in the head and neck region often involves an endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure that is indirectly related to the Structure of intracranial artery because interventions on extracranial vessels can affect or be associated with intracranial arterial anatomy. The endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck itself is classified under the broader procedural category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), establishing a hierarchical relationship in which the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is a subset of general arterial endarterectomy. Understanding these anatomical, pathological, and procedural connections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate surgical or endovascular treatments involving intracranial and extracranial arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2587
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is a medical intervention that targets vascular structures and is categorized under the broader group of other procedures on vessels of the head and neck. Within this category, specific activities such as hemodynamic monitoring and vascular cannula insertion are recognized as distinct but related procedures. This hierarchical organization highlights that procedures on blood vessels encompass both monitoring of circulatory dynamics and the placement of vascular cannulas, and they are considered a subset of the larger collection of head and neck vascular procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2448
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery serves as the anatomical site where the pathological finding termed تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis) is identified, indicating that this form of arterial sclerosis occurs within the brain’s arterial architecture and is relevant for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: situates these processes in the basilar artery (Arteria basilaris) and the broader Structure of intracranial artery, which serves as a finding site for occlusion/stenosis, transient cerebral ischemia, and intracranial atherosclerosis, and as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy of head and neck vessels (endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck) under the broader procedure category endarterectomy (استئصال باطنة الشريان). This integrated network supports comprehensive assessment and management of precerebral arterial disease, its neurologic manifestations, and systemic consequences.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery is identified as the anatomical site where تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerosis) can be found. In addition, this intracranial arterial structure serves as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy of vessels of the head and neck, indicating that surgical removal of plaque from head‑and‑neck arteries may be relevant to or impact the intracranial arterial anatomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a child component, indicating that the basilar artery is a sub‑structure within the broader intracranial arterial system. This anatomical structure is identified as a finding site for تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis), highlighting its clinical relevance in the assessment of cerebrovascular disease. Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck is described as an indirect procedure site of the Structure of intracranial artery, meaning that surgical removal of the arterial intima in head‑and‑neck vessels may be performed in relation to this intracranial arterial region. The procedure Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck has a parent relationship with استئصال باطنة الشريان (general endarterectomy), placing the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy as a specialized form of the broader endarterectomy technique. Finally, استئصال باطنة الشريان has member cases classified under حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ) (arterial thrombosis and embolism cases), indicating that endarterectomy is a therapeutic option for patients presenting with arterial thrombotic or embolic events. Together, these relationships delineate how the intracranial arterial anatomy, associated pathological findings, and endarterectomy procedures are interconnected in the diagnosis and management of cerebrovascular and arterial thrombo‑embolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2371
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria femoralis is an important vascular structure that serves both diagnostic and therapeutic roles. It is the anatomical location where a لغط فخذي (thigh bruit) may be detected, indicating turbulent blood flow in the thigh region. Additionally, the arteria femoralis is the direct procedural site for performing a peripheral vascular bypass, a surgical technique used to restore adequate blood flow around occluded or diseased segments of the peripheral arteries. These connections underscore the arteria femoralis’s relevance in vascular assessment and in the execution of revascularization procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: The Vascular System is the anatomical region implicated in various cerebrovascular and arterial disorders. It is identified as the anatomic site associated with other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease, reflecting a range of poorly characterized cerebrovascular pathologies that involve the brain’s blood vessels. Additionally, the Vascular System is the site of تسلخ الشرايين, an arterial ulceration condition that denotes damage to the arterial wall. Recognizing the Vascular System as the common anatomical substrate for these conditions underscores its central role in the pathophysiology of cerebrovascular disease and arterial injury, informing diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery includes the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component. This intracranial arterial structure is a recognized site for the finding of تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerosis). Although direct surgical access to intracranial arteries is limited, endarterectomy of vessels of the head and neck is considered an indirect procedural approach that may affect the structure of intracranial artery. Understanding the relationships among the basilar artery, intracranial atherosclerotic disease, and head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is important for comprehensive cerebrovascular assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1984
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as a type of تروية دموية (blood perfusion) and is placed under the broader category of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck. The anatomical location referred to as cévy is identified as a procedure_site_of for تروية دموية, indicating that interventions described as Procedure on blood vessel are performed at the cévy site. This hierarchy connects the specific vascular procedure to its anatomical context and to the general group of head‑and‑neck vascular interventions, facilitating organized clinical documentation and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.content: to the more general term “انسداد الشرايين” (arterial occlusion). Anatomically, the “Structure of intracranial artery” contains the “arteria basilaris” as a child element; the basilar artery is a documented finding site for “occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries”, and embolic events described as “embolie; arteria basilaris” are likewise mapped to this occlusive condition. The intracranial arterial sclerosis finding “تصلب عصيدي في المخ” serves both as a finding site and as a mapped‑from condition for the same precerebral arterial occlusion/stenosis. Arabic terminology aligns with these concepts: “تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي” (basilar artery narrowing) is a specific expression of “occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries”; “انصمام الشريان المخي” (cerebral artery embolism) is an inverse‑ISA subtype of “embolie; arteria basilaris”; and “تضيق الشرايين” (arterial stenosis) is a broader category that includes “تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي”. Together, these morphological, etiological,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified is a broad hierarchical category that encompasses a variety of specialized medical procedure groups. Within this category, Adjunct Vascular System Procedures is classified not only as a child of the general “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified” but also as a sub‑category of other non‑or therapeutic cardiovascular procedures and of other procedures on vessels other than head and neck, indicating its relevance to vascular interventions that are neither purely therapeutic nor limited to the head and neck region. Computer assisted surgery [CAS] is likewise a child of Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified and is linked to several imaging‑related parent categories, including other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, magnetic resonance imaging, and other CT scan, reflecting its reliance on advanced radiologic guidance. Ocular diagnostic procedures are organized under uncategorized histopathology procedures, a parent category that also includes anatomy, indicating that eye histopathology forms part of a broader anatomical and pathological assessment framework.
